sources: Chapman Field Supervisor Description for Resume'.docx
meta.transcripts_note: Story language matches privacy-safe STAR summaries in Kelly_Peterson_Resume_Portfolio.docx (no raw transcripts in repo).
flags: [NEEDS CONFIRMATION: professional email for resume header and web contact]

summary: I’ve taught TK–8 special education for 26 years—mostly SDC and RSP/Inclusion—where services have to match co-taught blocks and real IEP minutes.
Chapman Field Supervisor: CalTPA and 13-TPE observations in schools, then completion recommendations—including non-approval when warranted.
signature_impact: Chapman evaluator: CalTPA + 13 TPEs evidence; recommends completion via approval or non-approval.
Olive (OUSD): SDC + RSP/Inclusion aligned with live co-teaching and scheduled IEP minutes.
Mission Hills English: moved a reluctant reader from avoidance to novel electives across two years (privacy-safe).
Anna Borba: earned two flagged students back into classwork; families rejoined school life after long absences (privacy-safe).
CUSD Riley volunteer: grade-wide Revolutionary War stations; weekly fifth-grade and SPED reading probes.
Chino tenure: fifth-grade Master Teacher/GLC, RTI, SELPA behavior team, science fair, SSC, textbook adoption.
Uses running lesson notes and district scores in IEP reviews so services change with the student.
Chapman debriefs: candidates leave with specific lesson fixes matched to flagged TPE lines.

experience.title: Education Specialist (SDC + RSP/Inclusion)
bullets: Mild/moderate Olive caseload: SDC and RSP/Inclusion across whole group, small groups, and pull-aside blocks.
MTSS/RTI + UDL: adjusts scaffolds when progress monitoring shows gains—or stalls.
Co-teaches with gen-ed teachers so inclusion minutes happen in actual lessons, not on paper only.
Keeps families in the loop on goals, behavior plans, and IEP dates before meetings, not after surprises.
Trains paras on transitions and classroom routines so students land ready to learn in each block.

experience.title: Field Supervisor, Special Education & Multiple Subject Credential Candidates
bullets: University field supervisor for Special Ed and Multiple Subject candidates through clinical practice and student teaching placements.
Feedback ties lessons, management, and data to all 13 TPEs and each CalTPA checkpoint.
Formal observations with pre- and post-conferences; evidence-based next steps candidates can try the next day.
Works with mentors and program leads on concerns, paperwork quality, and fit of each placement—confidentiality first.
Joins program meetings where teams decide whether candidates are ready for added classroom responsibility.
Submits completion recommendations—including non-approval when performance and documentation require it.

experience.title: Special Education Instructional Aide
bullets: Small-group and 1:1 aide work under the education specialist—kept students on IEP tasks and class routines.
Covered transitions, materials, and duty spots so the specialist could keep teaching blocks uninterrupted.

experience.title: Volunteer — Fifth-Grade Classroom & Special Education Support
bullets: Fifth-grade volunteer during a career pivot: reinforced lessons and classroom routines beside the classroom teacher.
Ran a full-grade Revolutionary War walk-through—stations, materials, and student rotations across fifth grade.
Weekly fifth-grade reading checks plus SPED reading probes teams used to adjust interventions.

experience.title: Junior High English Teacher
bullets: Junior high English building-wide; rewrote pacing and texts when the core left students stuck or disengaged.
Teamed with the school psychologist on cross-grade behavior and learning plans for complex students.
Coached speech, spelling, and creative writing teams for regional ACSI competitions.
Reluctant reader (privacy-safe): two years from avoidance to engagement; later picked novel electives in high school.
story_bullets: Reluctant reader (privacy-safe): two years from avoidance to engagement; later picked novel electives in high school.

experience.title: Elementary Teacher (grades 1–6); Master Teacher (fifth grade)
bullets: Elementary grades 1–6, including a 1/2 combo; fifth-grade Master Teacher/GLC beside the resource specialist.
RTI team; West End SELPA academic/behavior team; trained Conflict Managers schoolwide.
Science fair lead; SSC; textbook adoption; PQR training; Chino PD DARE partner; IB Early Years certified.
Two tough students (privacy-safe): daily check-ins, clear expectations, families re-engaged—both later called it formative.
story_bullets: Two tough students (privacy-safe): daily check-ins, clear expectations, families re-engaged—both later called it formative.

experience.title: Sixth Grade Teacher
bullets: Taught sixth grade daily, wrote curriculum supplements, and ran interventions for students below benchmark.
Science fair coordinator; co-taught DARE with Chino PD.

impact_stories.title: Student outcomes and family partnership

star.situation: Two students were frequently regarded as behavior concerns, with low trust in school and uneven support outside of school affecting engagement.
star.task: Improve engagement and belonging while partnering with families in a respectful, sustainable way.
star.action: Used structured daily check-ins to build trust before raising academic demands; reduced participation barriers when signals indicated basic needs gaps; used interest-based strategies to strengthen self-efficacy and school connection.
star.result: Both students showed stronger engagement and participation; caregivers became active participants in school events after a long gap. Years later, both students described the steady support as important to avoiding negative outcomes and building stability.

impact_stories.star_card_word_count_note: Website STAR text is capped at 160 words total (privacy-safe; no names; no disability labels).

star_web.situation: At Anna Borba, two students were often seen as behavior concerns, with low trust in school and uneven support outside of school.
star_web.task: Improve engagement while partnering with families in a steady, respectful way.
star_web.action: Built trust through structured daily check-ins before raising academic demands; reduced participation barriers when basic-needs signals appeared; used interest-based strategies to strengthen confidence and connection.
star_web.result: Engagement and participation strengthened; caregivers began participating in school events after a long gap. Years later, both students described the consistent support as important to avoiding negative outcomes and building stability.

impact_stories.title: From reading avoidance to elective novel study

star.situation: A student arrived with strong reading avoidance; a caregiver communicated low expectations that the student would become a reader.
star.task: Rebuild confidence without turning reading into a high-stakes performance task on day one.
star.action: Prioritized scaffolded wins, visible early success, and a consistent “you can do this” stance across two school years rather than a remediation-only tone.
star.result: The student moved from avoidance to genuine engagement and self-directed reading interest; later enrolled in high school novel-study electives and credited the earlier confidence foundation as the reason they were willing to try.

impact_stories.star_card_word_count_note: Website STAR text is capped at 160 words total (privacy-safe; no names; no disability labels).

star_web.situation: In junior high English, a student arrived with strong reading avoidance, and a caregiver communicated low expectations that the student would become a reader.
star_web.task: Rebuild confidence without making early reading feel like a high-stakes performance task.
star_web.action: Used scaffolded wins and visible early success across two school years, with a consistent strengths-based stance rather than a remediation-only tone.
star_web.result: The student moved from avoidance toward engagement and self-directed reading interest; in high school, the student chose novel-study electives and credited the earlier confidence foundation as the reason they were willing to try.
